author: The Fourth Ecumenical Council. The Council of Chalcedon
book: The XXX Canons of the Holy and Fourth Synods, of Chalcedon.

classification.cautionReason: Conciliar or creedal material received in mainstream Nicene Christian tradition, with denominational differences on some canons.

lineage: The Fourth Ecumenical Council. The Council of Chalcedon.
The XXX Canons of the Holy and Fourth Synods, of Chalcedon.

metadata.author: The Fourth Ecumenical Council. The Council of Chalcedon

title: Canon XII.

verses.book: The XXX Canons of the Holy and Fourth Synods, of Chalcedon.
verses.text: It has come to our knowledge that certain persons, contrary to the laws of the Church, having had recourse to secular powers, have by means of imperial rescripts divided one Province into two, so that there are consequently two metropolitans in one province; therefore the holy Synod has decreed that for the future no such thing shall be attempted by a bishop, since he who shall undertake it shall be degraded from his rank.

verses.book: The XXX Canons of the Holy and Fourth Synods, of Chalcedon.
verses.text: But the cities which have already been honoured by means of imperial letters with the name of metropolis, and the bishops in charge of them, shall take the bare title, all metropolitan rights being preserved to the true Metropolis.

verses.book: The XXX Canons of the Holy and Fourth Synods, of Chalcedon.
verses.text: Ancient Epitome of Canon XII.

verses.book: The XXX Canons of the Holy and Fourth Synods, of Chalcedon.
verses.text: One province shall not be cut into two.

verses.book: The XXX Canons of the Holy and Fourth Synods, of Chalcedon.
verses.text: Whoever shall do this shall be cast out of the episcopate.

verses.book: The XXX Canons of the Holy and Fourth Synods, of Chalcedon.
verses.text: Such cities as are cut off by imperial rescript shall enjoy only the honour of having a bishop settled in them: but all the rights pertaining to the true metropolis shall be preserved.

verses.book: The XXX Canons of the Holy and Fourth Synods, of Chalcedon.
verses.text: We learn from this canon, there were cases in which an ambitious prelate, “by making application to the government” (“secular powers”) had obtained what are called “pragmatic letters,” and employed them for the purpose of “dividing one province into two,” and exalting himself as a metropolitan.

verses.book: The XXX Canons of the Holy and Fourth Synods, of Chalcedon.
verses.text: The name of a “pragmatic sanction” is more familiar in regard to medieval and modern history; it recalls the name of St. Louis, and, still more, that of the Emperor Charles VI. the father of Maria Theresa.

verses.book: The XXX Canons of the Holy and Fourth Synods, of Chalcedon.
verses.text: Properly a “pragmatic” was a deliberate order promulgated by the Emperor after full hearing of advice, on some public affair.

verses.book: The XXX Canons of the Holy and Fourth Synods, of Chalcedon.
verses.text: We find “pragmatici nostri statuta” in a law of a.d.

verses.book: The XXX Canons of the Holy and Fourth Synods, of Chalcedon.
verses.text: 1, 36); and “pragmatici prioris,” “sub hac pragmatica jussione,” in ordinances in Append. to Cod.

verses.book: The XXX Canons of the Holy and Fourth Synods, of Chalcedon.
verses.text: 95, 162; and the empress Pulcheria, about a year before the Council, had informed Leo that her husband Marcian had recalled some exiled orthodox bishops “robore pragmatici sui” (Leon., Epist. lxxvij.).

verses.book: The XXX Canons of the Holy and Fourth Synods, of Chalcedon.
verses.text: Justinian speaks of “pragmaticas nostras formas” and “pragmaticum typum” (Novel., 7, 9, etc.).

verses.book: The XXX Canons of the Holy and Fourth Synods, of Chalcedon.
verses.text: The phrase was adopted from his legislation by Louis the Pious and his colleague-son Lothar (compare Novel.

verses.book: The XXX Canons of the Holy and Fourth Synods, of Chalcedon.
verses.text: 7, 2 with Pertz, Mon.

verses.book: The XXX Canons of the Holy and Fourth Synods, of Chalcedon.
verses.text: Leg., i., 254), and hence it came to be used both by later German emperors (see, e.g., Bryce’s Holy Roman Empire, p.

verses.book: The XXX Canons of the Holy and Fourth Synods, of Chalcedon.
verses.text: 212), and by the French kings (Kitchin, Hist.

verses.book: The XXX Canons of the Holy and Fourth Synods, of Chalcedon.
verses.text: Augustine explains it by “præceptum imperatoris” (Brev.

verses.book: The XXX Canons of the Holy and Fourth Synods, of Chalcedon.
verses.text: Collat. cum Donatist. iii., 2), and Balsamon in his comment uses an equivalent phrase; and so in the record of the fourth session of Chalcedon we have θεῖα γράμματα (“divine” being practically equivalent to “imperial”) explained by πραγματικοὺς τύπους (Mansi, vii., 89).

verses.book: The XXX Canons of the Holy and Fourth Synods, of Chalcedon.
verses.text: We must observe that the imperial order, in the cases contemplated by the canon, had only conferred the title of “metropolis” on the city, and had not professed to divide the province for civil, much less for ecclesiastical, purposes.

verses.book: The XXX Canons of the Holy and Fourth Synods, of Chalcedon.
verses.text: Valens, indeed, had divided the province of Cappadocia, when in 371 he made Tyana a metropolis: and therefore Anthimus, bishop of Tyana, when he claimed the position of a metropolitan, with authority over suffragans, was making a not unnatural inference in regard to ecclesiastical limits from political rearrangements of territory, as Gregory of Nazianzus says (Orat. xliii., 58), whereas Basil “held to the old custom,” i.e., to the traditional unity of his provincial church, although after a while he submitted to what he could not hinder (see Tillemont, ix., 175, 182, 670).

verses.book: The XXX Canons of the Holy and Fourth Synods, of Chalcedon.
verses.text: But in the case of Eustathius of Berytus, which was clearly in the Council’s mind, the Phœnician province had not been divided; it was in reliance on a mere title bestowed upon his city, and also on an alleged synodical ordinance which issued in fact from the so-called “Home Synod” that he declared himself independent of his metropolitan, Photius of Tyre, and brought six bishoprics under his assumed jurisdiction.

verses.book: The XXX Canons of the Holy and Fourth Synods, of Chalcedon.
verses.text: Thus while the province remained politically one, he had de facto divided it ecclesiastically into two.

verses.book: The XXX Canons of the Holy and Fourth Synods, of Chalcedon.
verses.text: Photius petitioned Marcian, who referred the case to the Council of Chalcedon, and it was taken up in the fourth session.

verses.book: The XXX Canons of the Holy and Fourth Synods, of Chalcedon.
verses.text: The imperial commissioners announced that it was to be settled not according to “pragmatic forms,” but according to those which had been enacted by the Fathers (Mansi, vii., 89).

verses.book: The XXX Canons of the Holy and Fourth Synods, of Chalcedon.
verses.text: This encouraged the Council to say, “A pragmatic can have no force against the canons.”

verses.book: The XXX Canons of the Holy and Fourth Synods, of Chalcedon.
verses.text: The commissioners asked whether it was lawful for bishops, on the ground of a pragmatic, to steal away the rights of other churches?

verses.book: The XXX Canons of the Holy and Fourth Synods, of Chalcedon.
verses.text: The answer was explicit: “No, it is against the canon.”

verses.book: The XXX Canons of the Holy and Fourth Synods, of Chalcedon.
verses.text: The Council proceeded to cancel the resolution of the Home Synod in favour of the elevation of Berytus, ordered the 4th Nicene canon to be read, and upheld the metropolitical rights of Tyre.

verses.book: The XXX Canons of the Holy and Fourth Synods, of Chalcedon.
verses.text: The commissioners also pronounced against Eustathius.

verses.book: The XXX Canons of the Holy and Fourth Synods, of Chalcedon.
verses.text: Cecropius, bishop of Sebastopolis, requested them to put an end to the issue of pragmatics made to the detriment of the canons; the Council echoed this request; and the commissioners granted it by declaring that the canons should everywhere stand good (Mansi, vii., 89–97).

verses.book: The XXX Canons of the Holy and Fourth Synods, of Chalcedon.
verses.text: We may connect with this incident a law of Marcian dated in 454, by which “all pragmatic sanctions, obtained by means of favour or ambition in opposition to the canon of the Church, are declared to be deprived of effect” (Cod.

verses.book: The XXX Canons of the Holy and Fourth Synods, of Chalcedon.
verses.text: Justin, i., 2, 12).

verses.book: The XXX Canons of the Holy and Fourth Synods, of Chalcedon.
verses.text: To this decision the present canon looks back, when it forbids any bishop, on pain of deposition, to presume to do as Eustathius had done, since it decrees that “he who attempts to do so shall fall from his own rank (βαθμοῦ) in the Church.

verses.book: The XXX Canons of the Holy and Fourth Synods, of Chalcedon.
verses.text: And cities which have already obtained the honorary title of a metropolis from the emperor are to enjoy the honour only, and their bishops to be but honorary metropolitans, so that all the rights of the real metropolis are to be reserved to it.”

verses.book: The XXX Canons of the Holy and Fourth Synods, of Chalcedon.
verses.text: So, at the end of the 6th session the emperor had announced that Chalcedon was to be a titular metropolis, saving all the rights of Nicomedia; and the Council had expressed its assent (Mansi, xii., 177; cf.

verses.book: The XXX Canons of the Holy and Fourth Synods, of Chalcedon.
verses.text: Le Quien, i., 602).

verses.book: The XXX Canons of the Holy and Fourth Synods, of Chalcedon.
verses.text: Another case was discussed in the 13th session of the Council.

verses.book: The XXX Canons of the Holy and Fourth Synods, of Chalcedon.
verses.text: Anastasius of Nicæa had claimed to be independent of his metropolitan Eunomius of Nicomedia, on the ground of an ordinance of Valens, recognising the city of Nicæa as by old custom a “metropolis.”

verses.book: The XXX Canons of the Holy and Fourth Synods, of Chalcedon.
verses.text: Eunomius, who complained of Anastasius’s encroachments, appealed to a later ordinance, guaranteeing to the capital of Bithynia its rights as unaffected by the honour conferred on Nicæa: the Council expressed its mind in favour of Eunomius, and the dispute was settled by a decision “that the bishop of Nicomedia should have metropolitical authority over the Bithynian churches, while the bishop of Nicæa should have merely the honour of a metropolitan, being subjected, like the other comprovincials, to the bishop of Nicomedia (Mansi, vii., 313).

verses.book: The XXX Canons of the Holy and Fourth Synods, of Chalcedon.
verses.text: Zonaras says that this canon was in his time no longer observed; and Balsamon says that when the primates of Heraclea and Ancyra cited it as upholding their claim to perform the consecration of two “honorary metropolitans,” they were overruled by a decree of Alexius Comnenus, “in presence and with consent” of a synod (on Trullan, canon xxxviij.).

verses.book: The XXX Canons of the Holy and Fourth Synods, of Chalcedon.
verses.text: The first part of this canon is found in the Corpus Juris Canonici, Gratian’s Decretum, Pars I., Dist. ci., canon j.
